preamble: The transformation from a voiced aspirated bilabial plosive (bʱ) to a voiceless aspirated bilabial plosive (pʰ) is a historical phonetic shift primarily characterized by devoicing while maintaining aspiration. This change involves the vocal cords ceasing to vibrate during the articulation of the stop, resulting in a voiceless sound. Such a shift is attested in the development of certain Indo-European languages, where Proto-Indo-European voiced aspirated stops underwent devoicing in specific contexts.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing, Fortition (specifically, loss of voicing while maintaining aspiration)

examples.note: The Proto-Indo-European voiced aspirated stop *bʰ devoiced to a voiceless aspirated stop pʰ in initial positions in Ancient Greek and Proto-Italic.

sources: Faliscan Reflexes of the Indo-European voiced aspirates - Wolfgang D. C. de Melo
